---
title: "HIKET --- Bayesian Calibration of Forest SOC Models"
subtitle: "Methods and pipeline documentation"
author: "Lorenzo Menichetti"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %Y')`"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    latex_engine: xelatex
    extra_dependencies: ["booktabs", "amsmath", "longtable", "array", "siunitx"]
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{tikz}
  - \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
geometry: margin=2.5cm
fontsize: 11pt
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Overview

## Research question

Finnish forest soils show a positive trend in mineral SOC stocks from the
first systematic measurement campaign (VMI8, 1985--86) through Biosoil
(2006) and into the Komeetta campaign (2024). The trend is modest but
consistent, and its attribution is politically consequential: Finland
reports it as a genuine sink, whereas comparable northern countries do not.

Existing SOC decomposition models, when run with published parameter
sets or calibrated with standard steady-state assumptions, tend to
predict SOC saturation or decline that is difficult to reconcile with
the observed accumulation. This raises the central question of HIKET:

\begin{quote}
\emph{Do divergent model projections of Finnish forest SOC --- including
apparent saturation --- reflect genuine structural differences between
models, or artefacts of how the models are calibrated?}
\end{quote}

HIKET addresses this by calibrating six models against the same Finnish
data under identical likelihood, error, filtering, and --- crucially ---
initialisation assumptions. Residual differences among models are then
attributable to structure rather than to parametric choices. A key
innovation is the \emph{transient initialisation} approach (§\ref{transient}),
which gives all models a physically grounded, calibrated initial state
in 1985, rather than the analytically-imposed steady state traditionally
assumed. This single change substantially improves each model's ability
to track the observed SOC trajectory.

## Models

Six models span a complexity ladder crossed with two climate-integration
choices:

| Model    | Pools | Free params | Climate $\xi$                     | Notes                          |
|----------|------:|------------:|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| SP1      | 1     | 6           | single triplet, Yasso07 form      | Pure R                         |
| TP2      | 2     | 8           | single triplet, Yasso07 form      | ICBM topology, pure R          |
| TP3      | 3     | 10          | single triplet, Yasso07 form      | ASH cascade, pure R            |
| Yasso07  | 5     | 20          | single triplet                    | Fortran core                   |
| Yasso15  | 5     | 26          | pool-group-specific               | Fortran core (yasso15.so)      |
| Yasso20  | 5     | 26          | pool-group-specific, monthly $T$  | Same Fortran as Yasso15        |

Two comparison axes run through this design. First, a \emph{pool
complexity} axis: SP1 $\to$ TP2 $\to$ TP3 $\to$ Yasso07 increases the
number of pools from 1 to 5 while maintaining the same Yasso07 climate
response, isolating the effect of structural complexity on the
calibrated SOC trajectory. Second, a \emph{climate-integration} axis:
Yasso07 $\to$ Yasso15 $\to$ Yasso20 adds pool-group-specific climate
responses and finer intra-annual temperature integration, isolating
the effect of climate-coupling refinements.

All models share the same AWEN litter fractionation, observation error
model, likelihood form, and transient initialisation protocol. Residual
differences in calibrated trajectories are therefore structural.

## Pipeline

The pipeline runs in four sequential stages. Data preparation is
documented separately in `Data_work.R`.

| Script                                  | Stage       | Purpose                                          |
|-----------------------------------------|-------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `run_{Model}_baseline.R`                | 0 — Baseline | Forward run at published defaults; no calibration |
| `run_{Model}_transient_calibration.R`  | 1 — Calibration | MCMC posterior sampling                      |
| `run_{Model}_transient_predictive.R`   | 2 — Predictive  | Posterior predictive trajectories, residuals |
| `run_residual_analysis.R`              | 3 — Residuals   | Random-forest residual analysis              |
| `run_multimodel_comparison.R`          | 4 — Comparison  | Cross-model metrics and overlay figures      |

Shared MCMC settings are defined in `calib_config.R`. Prior
specifications live in `Prior_specs/{Model}_priors.R`, sourced at the
top of each calibration script.

---

# Data

A brief data description is given here; the complete treatment is
documented in `Data_work.R`.

## Plots and SOC observations

The calibration dataset covers approximately 350--400 permanent Finnish
NFI plots classified as calibration-ready
(`calib_ready = TRUE` in `site_raw.csv`). Plots are excluded if they
have zero or missing litter inputs, absent organic-layer measurements
(OFH), or implied mean residence times exceeding 100 years.

Three SOC measurement campaigns provide the calibration targets:

| Campaign  | Year(s)   | Notes                                        |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------------------------------|
| VMI8      | 1985--86  | First national campaign; mineral SOC depth   |
| Biosoil   | 2006      | European network; same depth protocol        |
| Komeetta  | 2024      | Third Finnish campaign; confirms saturation  |

With three temporally separated observations per plot, the calibration
constrains both the 1985--2006 accumulation phase and the subsequent
2006--2024 near-stability. This separation is important: models that
happen to fit the first interval but diverge over the second are
penalised.

## Litter inputs

Annual AWEN-fractionated litter inputs (tC ha$^{-1}$ yr$^{-1}$) come
from the plot-level dataset of Tupek et al.
(Zenodo, DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19736499). These are plot-level estimates
and are approximately flat over the observation period --- a key
empirical constraint discussed in §\ref{transient}.

## Climate

Gridded daily climate data (temperature, precipitation) span 1961--2025
(`nfi_plot_weather_data_1961_2025.nc`). Annual or monthly summaries
are computed per plot according to each model's climate-integration
requirements.

---

# Models

All six models follow first-order kinetics: each pool $C_i$ decomposes
at a rate proportional to its size, modulated by a dimensionless climate
modifier $\xi \geq 0$. Litter input $J$ is AWEN-fractionated (acid-,
water-, ethanol-, non-soluble) and the fractionation is fixed from the
input data.

## SP1 (single pool)\label{models-sp1}

All litter inputs --- NWL, FWL, and CWL summed across AWEN fractions
--- enter a single pool $C$:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = J(t) - \alpha\, \xi(t)\, C(t).$$

The annual step has a closed-form solution (piecewise-constant $J$ and
$\xi$ within each year):

$$C(t+1) = C_{\mathrm{ss}}(t) + \bigl(C(t) - C_{\mathrm{ss}}(t)\bigr)
  \,e^{-\alpha \xi(t)}, \qquad C_{\mathrm{ss}}(t) = \frac{J(t)}{\alpha\, \xi(t)}.$$

Pure R; no Fortran.

## TP2 (two pools)

Two pools, $A$ (active) and $H$ (humus). All litter enters $A$; $H$
receives transferred mass from $A$ only:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = J(t) - \alpha_A \xi(t) A(t),
  \qquad \frac{dH}{dt} = p_H \alpha_A \xi(t) A(t) - \alpha_H \xi(t) H(t).$$

This is the ICBM topology (Andrén \& Kätterer 1997) with $\xi$
multiplying both pool decay rates. Parameter naming follows Yasso07,
making the TP2 parameter set a strict subset of Yasso07. The annual step
is solved exactly (see §\ref{models-sp1}), not Euler-approximated. Pure R.

## TP3 (three pools)

Three pools in a strict sequential cascade: Active ($A$) $\to$ Slow
($S$) $\to$ Humus ($H$). All litter enters $A$; a fraction $p_S$ of $A$
decomposition transfers to $S$; a fraction $p_H$ of $S$ decomposition
transfers to $H$. $H$ has no climate modifier (consistent with TP2 and
Yasso conventions). The discrete annual dynamics are:

$$A(t+1) = A(t)\bigl(1 - \alpha_A\,\xi(t)\bigr) + J(t)$$
$$S(t+1) = S(t)\bigl(1 - \alpha_S\,\xi(t)\bigr) + p_S\,\alpha_A\,\xi(t)\,A(t)$$
$$H(t+1) = H(t)\bigl(1 - \alpha_H\bigr) + p_H\,\alpha_S\,\xi(t)\,S(t).$$

The Euler stepping is intentional: it matches the stepping used in the
runtime simulation, so the initialisation pre-run and the forward run
are numerically consistent. Analytical steady states exist and are used
as the starting point for the transient pre-run:

$$A^* = \frac{J}{\alpha_A\,\xi}, \qquad
  S^* = \frac{p_S J}{\alpha_S\,\xi}, \qquad
  H^* = \frac{p_H p_S J}{\alpha_H}.$$

TP3 sits between TP2 and Yasso07 on the complexity ladder, adding a
slow intermediate pool but retaining a sequential (no back-transfer)
topology. Pure R.

## The Yasso model family: shared structure

Yasso07, Yasso15, and Yasso20 share the same five-pool AWEN+H topology
and the same Fortran matrix-exponential solver. They differ only in how
the climate modifier $\xi$ is computed (§\ref{models-yasso07}--\ref{models-yasso20}).
This subsection describes the shared architecture for reference.

\subsubsection*{Pool structure and notation}

The five pools are: \textbf{A} (acid-hydrolysable), \textbf{W}
(water-soluble), \textbf{E} (ethanol-soluble), \textbf{N} (non-soluble,
largely lignin), and \textbf{H} (humus). Litter enters the four AWEN
pools in proportions fixed by the chemical fractionation of the input
data. Carbon flows between AWEN pools at rates $p_{ij} \cdot \alpha_j \cdot \xi \cdot C_j$,
where $p_{ij}$ is the fraction of pool $j$'s decomposition flux
routed to pool $i$. A fixed fraction $p_H$ of each AWEN pool's
decomposition flux forms humus; the remainder is respired as CO$_2$.
Humus decomposes irreversibly at rate $\alpha_H$ (no climate modifier
in Yasso07; pool-group-specific $\xi_H$ in Yasso15/20).

\subsubsection*{Pool diagram}

The diagram below shows all pools, litter inputs, inter-pool transfers
(labelled with HIKET parameter names), humification, and respiration
pathways. Litter inputs $J_A, J_W, J_E, J_N$ are AWEN fractions of the
annual plot-level litter; for FWL and CWL cohorts a woody-size modifier
$h_S(d)$ additionally scales $\alpha_j$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style  = {rectangle, rounded corners=4pt, draw=black!80, thick,
                 minimum width=1.15cm, minimum height=0.85cm,
                 font=\sffamily\bfseries\small, fill=white},
  hbox/.style = {box, fill=gray!15},
  inp/.style  = {-{Stealth[length=5pt]}, thick, blue!65!black},
  tr/.style   = {-{Stealth[length=4pt]}, thick, black!75},
  hum/.style  = {-{Stealth[length=4pt]}, thick, gray!70, dashed},
  rsp/.style  = {-{Stealth[length=4pt]}, thick, red!55!black, dotted},
  lbl/.style  = {font=\tiny\sffamily, fill=white, inner sep=0.8pt,
                 text=black!80}
]

%% --- Pool nodes ---
\node[box]  (A) at (0.0, 3.6) {A};
\node[box]  (W) at (4.2, 3.6) {W};
\node[box]  (E) at (0.0, 0.0) {E};
\node[box]  (N) at (4.2, 0.0) {N};
\node[hbox] (H) at (2.1,-2.4) {H};

%% --- Litter inputs ---
\draw[inp] (-1.5, 3.6) -- (A) node[midway, above, lbl] {$J_A$};
\draw[inp] (2.1,  5.3) -- (W) node[midway, right, lbl] {$J_W$};
\draw[inp] (-1.5, 0.0) -- (E) node[midway, above, lbl] {$J_E$};
\draw[inp] (5.7,  0.0) -- (N) node[midway, above, lbl] {$J_N$};

%% --- A <-> W (top horizontal) ---
\draw[tr, bend left=13]  (A) to node[lbl, above] {$p_{AW}$} (W);
\draw[tr, bend left=13]  (W) to node[lbl, below] {$p_{WA}$} (A);

%% --- E <-> N (bottom horizontal) ---
\draw[tr, bend right=13] (E) to node[lbl, below] {$p_{EN}$} (N);
\draw[tr, bend right=13] (N) to node[lbl, above] {$p_{NE}$} (E);

%% --- A <-> E (left vertical) ---
\draw[tr, bend right=13] (A) to node[lbl, left]  {$p_{AE}$} (E);
\draw[tr, bend right=13] (E) to node[lbl, right] {$p_{EA}$} (A);

%% --- W <-> N (right vertical) ---
\draw[tr, bend left=13]  (W) to node[lbl, right] {$p_{WN}$} (N);
\draw[tr, bend left=13]  (N) to node[lbl, left]  {$p_{NW}$} (W);

%% --- A <-> N (diagonal) ---
\draw[tr, bend left=7]  (A) to node[lbl, above right, yshift=2pt] {$p_{AN}$} (N);
\draw[tr, bend left=7]  (N) to node[lbl, below left,  yshift=-2pt]{$p_{NA}$} (A);

%% --- W <-> E (diagonal) ---
\draw[tr, bend right=7] (W) to node[lbl, above left,  yshift=2pt] {$p_{WE}$} (E);
\draw[tr, bend right=7] (E) to node[lbl, below right, yshift=-2pt]{$p_{EW}$} (W);

%% --- Humification: AWEN -> H ---
\draw[hum] (A.south) to[out=-80, in=145]
           node[lbl, left, pos=0.35] {$p_H$} (H.north west);
\draw[hum] (W.south) to[out=-100,in=35]
           node[lbl, right,pos=0.35] {$p_H$} (H.north east);
\draw[hum] (E.east)  to[out=-20, in=175]
           node[lbl, below,pos=0.5] {$p_H$} (H.west);
\draw[hum] (N.west)  to[out=200, in=5]
           node[lbl, below,pos=0.5] {$p_H$} (H.east);

%% --- H respiration ---
\draw[rsp] (H.south) -- +(0,-0.9)
           node[below, font=\tiny\sffamily] {CO$_2$\;($\alpha_H$)};

%% --- AWEN respiration ---
\draw[rsp] (A.north west) -- +(-0.7, 0.7)
           node[above left, font=\tiny\sffamily] {CO$_2$};
\draw[rsp] (W.north east) -- +( 0.7, 0.7)
           node[above right,font=\tiny\sffamily] {CO$_2$};
\draw[rsp] (E.south west) -- +(-0.7,-0.7)
           node[below left, font=\tiny\sffamily] {CO$_2$};
\draw[rsp] (N.south east) -- +( 0.7,-0.7)
           node[below right,font=\tiny\sffamily] {CO$_2$};

%% --- xi annotation ---
\node[font=\scriptsize\sffamily, align=center, text=black!60]
  at (2.1, 4.8)
  {$\xi$: climate modifier on $\alpha_{A,W,E,N}$
   (and $\alpha_H$ in Yasso15/20)};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3em}
\noindent
\textbf{Arrow key:}
\tikz[baseline=-0.5ex]{\draw[-{Stealth[length=4pt]},thick,black!75](0,0)--(0.6,0);}~inter-pool transfer ($p_{ij}$)\quad
\tikz[baseline=-0.5ex]{\draw[-{Stealth[length=4pt]},thick,gray!70,dashed](0,0)--(0.6,0);}~humification ($p_H$)\quad
\tikz[baseline=-0.5ex]{\draw[-{Stealth[length=4pt]},thick,red!55!black,dotted](0,0)--(0.6,0);}~respiration (CO$_2$)\quad
\tikz[baseline=-0.5ex]{\draw[-{Stealth[length=4pt]},thick,blue!65!black](0,0)--(0.6,0);}~litter input ($J$)

\subsubsection*{Parameter summary}

\begin{small}
\begin{longtable}{l l l l}
\toprule
Parameter & Symbol & Role & Models \\
\midrule
Decomp.\ rates (AWEN) & $\alpha_A, \alpha_W, \alpha_E, \alpha_N$ &
  Base first-order rate of each AWEN pool (yr$^{-1}$); fixed at published values & Y07/15/20 \\
Decomp.\ rate (H) & $\alpha_H$ &
  Humus decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$); fixed & Y07/15/20 \\
Transfer fractions & $p_{ij}$, $i \neq j \in \{A,W,E,N\}$ &
  Fraction of pool $j$ decomp.\ flux routed to pool $i$; 12 per model, all free & Y07/15/20 \\
Humification & $p_H$ &
  Fraction of each AWEN decomp.\ flux routed to H; same for all four pools; fixed & Y07/15/20 \\
AWE climate, linear & $\beta_1$ &
  Linear temperature coefficient in $\xi_\mathrm{AWE}$; free, log-transformed & Y07/15/20 \\
AWE climate, quadratic & $\beta_2$ &
  Quadratic temperature coefficient; free, unconstrained & Y07/15/20 \\
AWE precipitation & $\gamma$ &
  Precipitation saturation exponent in $\xi_\mathrm{AWE}$; free, unconstrained & Y07/15/20 \\
N-pool climate & $\beta_{N1}, \beta_{N2}, \gamma_N$ &
  As above, for the N pool separately & Y15/20 \\
H-pool climate & $\beta_{H1}, \beta_{H2}, \gamma_H$ &
  As above, for the H pool separately & Y15/20 \\
Size modifier & $\delta_1, \delta_2, r$ &
  Reduce $\alpha$ for woody cohorts: $h_S(d) = \min(1,(1+\delta_1 d + \delta_2 d^2)^{-|r|})$; free & Y07/15/20 \\
Leaching & $w_1,\ldots,w_5$ &
  Leaching loss weights; inactive (leac\,=\,0) throughout; fixed & Y15/20 \\
Historical litter & $\sigma_\mathrm{init}$ &
  Scales 1917 litter anchor in the pre-run; encodes pre-1985 productivity history; free & all \\
Contemporary litter & $\sigma_\mathrm{input}$ &
  Scales observed litter inputs (1985 onward); absorbs litter model uncertainty; free & all \\
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}
\end{small}
\vspace{-0.5em}
{\footnotesize Y07 = Yasso07; Y15 = Yasso15; Y20 = Yasso20; Y15/20 = both.}

## Yasso07\label{models-yasso07}

Five pools (Tuomi et al. 2009, 2011): A (acid-soluble), W
(water-soluble), E (ethanol-soluble), N (non-soluble / lignin), H
(humus). Litter inputs enter A, W, E, N proportionally to AWEN
fractions; transfers between AWEN pools are controlled by 12 fractions
$p_{ij}$; all four AWEN pools contribute to H at a humification rate
$p_H$; H decomposes irreversibly at rate $\alpha_H$.

The dynamics are $dC/dt = AC + b(t)$, with the matrix $A$ assembled
from decomposition rates and transfer fractions. The Fortran core
solves each annual step analytically via the matrix exponential
$C(t+1) = A^{-1}(e^{A\Delta t}(AC(t) + b) - b)$, with a Euler fallback
for near-singular matrices. NWL, FWL, and CWL litter types are tracked
as three parallel cohorts; the per-cohort state vector has 15 elements
(5 pools $\times$ 3 cohorts).

The climate modifier uses a four-point Gaussian approximation of the
intra-annual temperature cycle:

$$\xi(\bar T, T_{\mathrm{amp}}, P) =
  \frac{1}{4}\sum_{k=1}^{4} \exp\!\bigl(\beta_1 T_k + \beta_2 T_k^2\bigr)
  \times \bigl(1 - \exp(\gamma P/1000)\bigr),$$

where $T_k$ are four quarterly temperatures derived from annual mean
$\bar T$ and seasonal amplitude $T_{\mathrm{amp}}$. A woody-size modifier
scales decomposition for FWL/CWL cohorts:

$$h_S(d) = \min\!\Bigl\{1,\; (1 + \delta_1 d + \delta_2 d^2)^{-|r|}\Bigr\},$$

where $d$ is the cohort diameter (cm; fixed at 2 cm for FWL,
15 cm for CWL).

## Yasso15

Identical pool topology, AWEN partitioning, and matrix-exponential
machinery as Yasso07. The structural change is \emph{pool-group-specific}
climate response: three triplets $(\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma)$,
$(\beta_{N1}, \beta_{N2}, \gamma_N)$, $(\beta_{H1}, \beta_{H2},
\gamma_H)$ act on the AWE, N, and H pool groups respectively. The
functional form matches Yasso07 (four-point Gaussian temperature
approximation, exponential-with-saturation precipitation response).
Leaching parameters $w_1, \ldots, w_5$ are present in the 35-element
parameter vector but are inactive throughout (leaching scalar held at
zero).

## Yasso20\label{models-yasso20}

Same Fortran core as Yasso15 (compiled as `yasso15.so`, reused). The
single structural change is the intra-annual temperature integration:
instead of the four-point Gaussian approximation, the modifier is
averaged directly over all 12 monthly temperatures:

$$\xi_T^{(g)} = \frac{1}{12}\sum_{m=1}^{12}
  \exp\!\bigl(\beta_1^{(g)} T_m + \beta_2^{(g)} T_m^2\bigr),
  \qquad g \in \{\mathrm{AWE}, N, H\}.$$

This is the only computational difference from Yasso15. Monthly climate
data (12 rows per year per plot) are required, compared to annual
summaries for all other models.

Yasso20 inherits the same 35-parameter structure as Yasso15 in this
calibration (all 12 inter-pool flow fractions free; see §\ref{param-yasso20}).

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Note on the published Yasso20 parameterisation.}
The MAP parameter values from the original global calibration (Viskari
et al. 2022) include six transfer fractions constrained to zero. When
these published values are applied directly, the per-pool mass budget
for pools A and N is marginally exceeded (transfer fractions plus
humification fraction sum slightly above 1.0), producing a numerically
unstable forward simulation in the HIKET Fortran implementation, which
enforces strict diagonal dominance. The HIKET calibration avoids this
region of parameter space entirely through the stick-breaking budget
constraint (§\ref{stick-breaking}). The Yasso20 uncalibrated baseline
uses FMI prior-centre values, which satisfy mass conservation.

---

# Fortran implementation: differences from the FMI reference code

The HIKET Fortran cores (`yasso07.f90`, `yasso15.f90`) derive from the
FMI Yasso implementations (Järvenpää 2015 for Yasso15/20; Tuomi et al.
for Yasso07) but incorporate several modifications introduced in 2025.
These are documented here for transparency and to assist cross-comparison
with the original FMI code.

## Diagonal dominance check and Euler fallback

The annual timestep in each model is solved via the matrix exponential
$\exp(A \Delta t)$. The original FMI code applies the matrix exponential
unconditionally. The HIKET implementation adds a pre-check for diagonal
dominance: for each column $j$ of $A$, if the sum of off-diagonal absolute
values (outflows to other pools and to humus) exceeds 99.99\% of the diagonal
absolute value (total decomposition rate), the matrix is classified as
near-singular and a first-order Euler step is used instead:

$$C(t+\Delta t) \approx \max\bigl(0,\; C(t) + (A\,C(t) + b)\,\Delta t\bigr).$$

The condition being detected is mathematically equivalent to the per-pool
mass budget $\sum_i p_{ij} + p_H \gtrsim 1$: the sum of all transfer
fractions and the humification fraction approaches or exceeds 1, leaving
zero or negative respiration from pool $j$. The $\max(0, \cdot)$ clamp
prevents negative pool values in the Euler path but does not conserve mass
exactly; repeated Euler steps with clamping can cause slow carbon drift
(see §\ref{models-yasso20} for the Yasso20 context). The HIKET calibration
avoids this regime entirely via the stick-breaking budget constraint.

## Matrix exponential: scaling cap and Taylor terms

The matrix exponential is computed by scaling-and-squaring with a Taylor
series expansion. Two changes were made to the original implementation:

* **Scaling-step cap.** The original code had no upper bound on the number
  of scaling steps $j$ (chosen so that $\|A\| < 2^j$). For ill-conditioned
  matrices with very large norms, $j$ grew without bound and the squaring
  loop ran for thousands of iterations, effectively hanging. The cap
  $j \leq 64$ (covering $\|A\| \leq 2^{64} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}$)
  terminates the scaling phase for any physically meaningful matrix.

* **Taylor terms.** The number of Taylor series terms was increased from 10
  to 20. When heavy scaling is applied (large $j$), the scaled matrix
  $A / 2^j$ is close to zero and many terms are needed for the expansion to
  accumulate the correction accurately.

## Terminal per-cohort state output (\texttt{C\_final})

The Fortran run routines (`yasso07_run_r`, `yasso15_run_r`) return a new
output argument `C_final(15)`: the terminal per-cohort pool state
$[C_\mathrm{nwl} \mid C_\mathrm{fwl} \mid C_\mathrm{cwl}]$ at the end of the
last simulated year. This argument is absent from the original FMI code,
which returns only the five summed pool totals.

`C_final` is required for two purposes in HIKET. First, the transient
pre-run chains directly into the calibration period: the pre-run's
terminal state becomes $C_\mathrm{init}$ for 1985 (§\ref{transient}).
Without the full 15-element per-cohort breakdown, re-initialising the
forward simulation would require an analytical steady-state approximation
that introduces additional error. Second, the 60-year forward projection
(predictive stage) chains from the end of the historical run in the same
way. The `C_final` field is attached to the R-side output data frame via
`attr(run_out, "C_final")`.

## $\xi$ precomputation in R

The original FMI code computes the climate modifier $\xi$ internally
within the Fortran timestep loop, from raw temperature and precipitation
inputs. HIKET separates this computation: $\xi$ is computed as an annual
(or monthly-aggregated) array in R \emph{before} the Fortran call, and
passed to the Fortran as a pre-computed vector argument. This separation
has two benefits. First, it allows the Jensen's inequality correction
(§\ref{transient}) to be applied consistently and transparently at the R
level. Second, it makes the $\xi$ functional form easily modifiable
without recompiling Fortran; the different forms for Yasso07 (four-point
Gaussian), Yasso15 (same form, pool-group-specific), and Yasso20
(12-monthly average) are handled in dedicated R functions
(`compute_xi_yasso07`, `compute_xi_yasso15`, `compute_xi_yasso20`).

## Floating-point precision (Yasso15 and Yasso20)

In `yasso15_mod`, the precision kind is defined as
`dp = SELECTED_REAL_KIND(6, 37)`, which evaluates to `REAL(4)`
(single precision, $\sim$7 significant digits) on standard hardware.
Despite the `REAL(dp)` declarations throughout the source, all Fortran
arguments for Yasso15 and Yasso20 are therefore 4-byte. The R wrappers
use `as.single()` for all `.Fortran()` calls to Yasso15/20.

Yasso07 uses `dp = SELECTED_REAL_KIND(15, 307)` = genuine double
precision. Its R wrapper correctly uses `as.double()`.

An earlier version of the Yasso15 wrapper incorrectly used `as.double()`
for the Yasso15/20 calls. This caused segfaults in the Fortran (type
mismatch in the `.Fortran()` interface) that were resolved by reverting
to `as.single()`.

## Parameter ordering

The FMI `.dat` posterior files use a different parameter ordering from
HIKET. In the FMI files, leaching weights $w_1$--$w_5$ occupy positions
17--21, followed by the climate parameters; in HIKET, climate parameters
occupy positions 17--25 and leaching weights are at 31--35. The remapping
is applied in `Priors_model_matching.R` via the index vector
`ORIG_TO_LORENZO` and verified by cross-checking MAP values against
named CSV files (`y15par.csv`, `y07par_gui.csv`).

## Yasso07: sign convention for $r$

The original Yasso07 parameter files store $r$ as a negative value
(e.g., $-0.307$). The Fortran applies $-|r|$ in the size modifier
exponent, so the sign is irrelevant at runtime. HIKET stores $r$ as
positive and applies a log transform in the calibration (which requires
positivity). The `abs()` conversion is applied when reading reference
values from `y07par_gui.csv` or `YASSO07_DEFAULT_PARAMS`.

---

# Parameter specification

## Free, fixed, and derived parameters

For each model, parameters split into:

* **Free**: sampled by MCMC. Includes the two auxiliary uncertainty
  parameters $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ and $\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$
  in every model.
* **Fixed**: held at published or structurally motivated values. For
  the Yasso family, decomposition rates and humus parameters are fixed
  at published defaults, reducing free-parameter count and keeping the
  intercomparison clean.
* **Derived**: none in the current pipeline.

Transforms map constrained physical parameters to an unconstrained
sampling space, with log-Jacobian contributions added to the prior
density:

* `log`: positivity; $x = \log\theta$.
* `logit`: $(0,1)$ bounds; $x = \log(\theta/(1-\theta))$.
* `unconstrained`: identity; no constraint beyond prior.
* `stick_break`: $K$-fraction simplex constrained to sum $\leq B$;
  described in §\ref{stick-breaking}.

## SP1 (6 free, 0 fixed)

| Parameter | Meaning | Transform |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| $\alpha$ | Decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$) | log |
| $\beta_1$ | Temperature response, linear | log |
| $\beta_2$ | Temperature response, quadratic | unconstrained |
| $\gamma$ | Precipitation response | unconstrained |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ | Historical litter scaling (1917 anchor) | log |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | Contemporary litter scaling | log |

## TP2 (8 free, 0 fixed)

| Parameter | Meaning | Transform |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| $\alpha_A$ | A-pool decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$) | log |
| $\alpha_H$ | H-pool decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$) | log |
| $p_H$ | Humification fraction (A $\to$ H) | logit |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$ | Climate response | log, unc., unc. |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | Litter scalings | log, log |

## TP3 (10 free, 0 fixed)

| Parameter | Meaning | Transform |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| $\alpha_A$ | A-pool decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$) | log |
| $\alpha_S$ | S-pool decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$) | log |
| $\alpha_H$ | H-pool decomposition rate (yr$^{-1}$) | log |
| $p_S$ | Transfer fraction A $\to$ S | logit |
| $p_H$ | Transfer fraction S $\to$ H | logit |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$ | Climate response | log, unc., unc. |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | Litter scalings | log, log |

No fixed parameters. Like SP1 and TP2, TP3 has no published global
calibration, so all parameters are learned from the Finnish data.

## Yasso07 (20 free, 6 fixed)

**Fixed (6):** $\alpha_A, \alpha_W, \alpha_E, \alpha_N$ (AWEN
decomposition rates), $p_H$, $\alpha_H$. Published Tuomi et al. defaults.

**Free (18):**

| Parameter | Count | Transform |
|-----------|------:|-----------|
| 12 transfer fractions $p_{ij}$, 4 columns of 3 | 12 | stick\_break |
| $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$, $\gamma$ | 3 | log, unc., unc. |
| $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$, $r$ | 3 | unc., log, log |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | 2 | log, log |

$\delta_2 > 0$ enforced (Fortran NaN for $\delta_2 < 0$ at $d > 0.4$
cm). $r$ stored as positive in HIKET; reference defaults store it
negative.

## Yasso15 (26 free, 11 fixed)

**Fixed (11):** $\alpha_A, \alpha_W, \alpha_E, \alpha_N$, $p_H$,
$\alpha_H$, $w_1, \ldots, w_5$ (leaching weights; inactive at leac = 0).
Published Yasso15 defaults.

**Free (28):**

| Parameter | Count | Transform |
|-----------|------:|-----------|
| 12 transfer fractions $p_{ij}$ | 12 | stick\_break |
| AWE climate: $\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$ | 3 | log, unc., unc. |
| N climate: $\beta_{N1}, \beta_{N2}, \gamma_N$ | 3 | log, unc., unc. |
| H climate: $\beta_{H1}, \beta_{H2}, \gamma_H$ | 3 | log, unc., unc. |
| Size modifier: $\delta_1, \delta_2, r$ | 3 | unc., log, log |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | 2 | log, log |

## Yasso20 (26 free, 11 fixed)\label{param-yasso20}

Identical fixed/free structure to Yasso15. The same 35-parameter vector
is used; only the $\xi$ computation method differs at runtime.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Uniform 12-fraction structure across the Yasso
family.} Earlier Yasso20 calibrations (Viskari 2022) fixed six transfer
fractions at zero (structural zeros from a global-dataset MCMC) and
derived three algebraically. HIKET does not impose these constraints.
All 12 inter-pool fractions are sampled in all three Yasso models,
for two reasons. First, the Viskari zeros reflect a warm-site global
calibration and are not general biological laws; Finnish boreal data
should be free to inform pool-flow structure. Second, fewer free
fractions in Yasso20 than in Yasso07 or Yasso15 would confound
structural (climate-integration) differences with parametric (degrees
of freedom) differences, making the intercomparison uninterpretable.
Prior centres for fractions use the published Yasso15 defaults (see §4).

## Stick-breaking parameterisation for transfer fractions\label{stick-breaking}

Each column $j$ of the AWEN transfer matrix has three outflow fractions
satisfying $\sum_i p_{ij} + p_H \leq 1$ (mass conservation; the unallocated
remainder $1 - \sum_i p_{ij} - p_H$ is respired as CO$_2$). The constraint
is enforced via stick-breaking with budget $B = 1 - p_H$. Three
unconstrained parameters $z_1, z_2, z_3$ map to the three fractions by
sequentially breaking pieces off the remaining budget:

$$p_1 = B \cdot \sigma(z_1), \quad
  p_2 = (B - p_1) \cdot \sigma(z_2), \quad
  p_3 = (B - p_1 - p_2) \cdot \sigma(z_3),$$

where $\sigma$ is the logistic function. Because each $\sigma(z_i) < 1$,
the sum $p_1 + p_2 + p_3$ is strictly less than $B$, guaranteeing a
strictly positive respired fraction. All three fractions in a column are
therefore free parameters --- none is algebraically determined --- and
the log-Jacobian of the transform is added to the prior. This guarantees
the budget constraint exactly and produces a well-conditioned Jacobian.
Each of the four AWEN columns uses an independent stick-breaking transform
with the same budget $B$, contributing $4 \times 3 = 12$ free parameters
for the 12 transfer fractions.

## Cross-model parameter overlap

\begin{small}
\begin{longtable}{l c c c c c c}
\toprule
Parameter & SP1 & TP2 & TP3 & Yasso07 & Yasso15 & Yasso20 \\
\midrule
$\alpha$ (single rate)             & free  & ---   & ---   & ---   & ---   & ---   \\
$\alpha_A, \alpha_H$               & ---   & free  & free  & fixed & fixed & fixed \\
$\alpha_S$                         & ---   & ---   & free  & ---   & ---   & ---   \\
$\alpha_W, \alpha_E, \alpha_N$     & ---   & ---   & ---   & fixed & fixed & fixed \\
$p_H$                              & ---   & free  & free  & fixed & fixed & fixed \\
$p_S$                              & ---   & ---   & free  & ---   & ---   & ---   \\
12 transfer fractions $p_{ij}$     & ---   & ---   & ---   & free  & free  & free  \\
$\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$         & free  & free  & free  & free  & free (AWE) & free (AWE) \\
$\beta_{N1}, \beta_{N2}, \gamma_N$ & ---   & ---   & ---   & ---   & free  & free  \\
$\beta_{H1}, \beta_{H2}, \gamma_H$ & ---   & ---   & ---   & ---   & free  & free  \\
$\delta_1, \delta_2, r$            & ---   & ---   & ---   & free  & free  & free  \\
$w_1, \ldots, w_5$                 & ---   & ---   & ---   & ---   & fixed & fixed \\
$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ & free & free & free & free & free & free \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total free} & \textbf{6} & \textbf{8} & \textbf{10} & \textbf{20} & \textbf{26} & \textbf{26} \\
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}
\end{small}

---

# Priors

All priors are normal in unconstrained (transformed) space, centred at
published model defaults. The joint prior factorises across parameters:

$$\log p(x) = \sum_{i} \log\mathcal{N}\!\bigl(x_i;\, x_i^{\mathrm{ctr}},
  \sigma_{\mathrm{ppm},i}\bigr) + \log J(x),$$

where $x^{\mathrm{ctr}}$ is the unconstrained image of the physical-space
prior centre, $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}}$ is the prior standard deviation in
unconstrained space, and $\log J$ is the log-Jacobian of the transforms.
Prior specifications are stored in `Prior_specs/{Model}_priors.R` and
sourced at the top of each calibration script.

## Sources

\begin{small}
\begin{longtable}{l l l}
\toprule
Model & Source for prior centres & Source for prior widths \\
\midrule
SP1   & Hand-set (no published global calib.) & Weakly informative \\
TP2   & Hand-set ICBM-style defaults          & Weakly informative \\
TP3   & Hand-set; extends TP2 with Slow pool  & Weakly informative \\
Yasso07 & \texttt{y07par\_gui.csv} (Tuomi et al.) & Literature CIs \\
Yasso15 & \texttt{Yasso15.dat} posterior mean (FMI) & \texttt{Yasso15.dat} posterior SD \\
Yasso20 & \texttt{Yasso20\_sample\_parameters.rda} (FMI) & \texttt{Yasso20.dat} posterior SD \\
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}
\end{small}

The FMI repository is at
\url{https://github.com/YASSOmodel/Ryassofortran/tree/master/data}.
The `.dat` files contain 10\,000 MCMC samples from the original
calibrations (row 1 = MAP, rows 2--10\,001 = posterior draws). Column
ordering in the FMI files differs from HIKET's parameter ordering;
remapping is performed and verified in `Priors_model_matching.R`.

For Yasso07, no posterior file is publicly available; $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}}$
is derived from the confidence intervals reported in Tuomi et al.
(2008--2010). For SP1, TP2, and TP3, priors are hand-set to plausible
starting values and wide enough to let the Finnish data dominate.

## Transfer-fraction priors

For all three Yasso models the prior standard deviation on every transfer
fraction is $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}} = 1.0$ in unconstrained (stick-breaking)
space. This is deliberately weakly informative: pool-flow structure is the
primary structural-comparison axis, and the Finnish data should drive it.
Prior centres for the fractions use the published Yasso15 defaults for
all three Yasso models (Yasso20 included, since the previously-fixed zeros
are now free parameters and Yasso15 defaults are the closest available
nonzero prior centres from the same chemical-fractionation framework).

## Climate-response and size-modifier priors

Climate and size parameters receive \emph{informative} priors, derived
from the FMI posterior (Yasso15/20) or from literature CIs (Yasso07).
This prevents two pathologies:

1. **The $\gamma$\,/\,$\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ compensation loop.** Both
   parameters scale total decomposition; without an informative prior on
   $\gamma$ they trade off freely, producing a ridge in the posterior that
   destabilises MCMC mixing.
2. **The $\delta_2 < 0$ NaN pathology.** The Fortran size modifier returns
   NaN for $\delta_2 < 0$ at $d > 0.4$ cm; a log transform with
   informative prior keeps $\delta_2$ in the well-behaved region.

$\gamma_H$ for Yasso15 is capped at $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}} = 1.5$: the
H-pool precipitation response is near-unidentifiable at Finnish
precipitation levels ($\sim 600$ mm). The large raw posterior SD ($\sim 3$)
reflects a flat likelihood surface, not genuine uncertainty.

## Numerical values

\textbf{Yasso07}

| Parameter | $x^{\mathrm{ctr}}$ (physical) | $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}}$ | Space |
|-----------|------------------------------:|------------------------:|-------|
| $p_{ij}$ (12) | published defaults | 1.0 | unconstrained |
| $\beta_1$ | 0.0987 | 0.20 | log |
| $\beta_2$ | $-0.00157$ | 0.05 | physical |
| $\gamma$ | $-1.272$ | 0.30 | physical |
| $\delta_1$ | $-1.708$ | 0.15 | physical |
| $\delta_2$ | 0.859 | 0.10 | log |
| $r$ | 0.307 | 0.015 | log |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | 1.00 | 0.50 | log |

\textbf{Yasso15}

| Parameter | $x^{\mathrm{ctr}}$ | $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}}$ | Space |
|-----------|-------------------:|------------------------:|-------|
| $p_{ij}$ (12) | published defaults | 1.0 | unconstrained |
| $\beta_1$ | 0.0906 | 0.0459 | log |
| $\beta_2$ | $-2.15 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.4 \times 10^{-4}$ | physical |
| $\gamma$ | $-1.809$ | 0.0702 | physical |
| $\beta_{N1}$ | 0.0488 | 0.105 | log |
| $\beta_{N2}$ | $-7.92 \times 10^{-5}$ | $8 \times 10^{-5}$ | physical |
| $\gamma_N$ | $-1.173$ | 0.155 | physical |
| $\beta_{H1}$ | 0.0352 | 0.135 | log |
| $\beta_{H2}$ | $-2.08 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.6 \times 10^{-4}$ | physical |
| $\gamma_H$ | $-12.54$ | 1.50 (capped) | physical |
| $\delta_1$ | $-0.439$ | 0.328 | physical |
| $\delta_2$ | 1.267 | 0.286 | log |
| $r$ | 0.257 | 0.055 | log |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | 1.00 | 0.50 | log |

\textbf{Yasso20}

| Parameter | $x^{\mathrm{ctr}}$ | $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}}$ | Space |
|-----------|-------------------:|------------------------:|-------|
| $p_{ij}$ (12) | Yasso15 defaults | 1.0 | unconstrained |
| $\beta_1$ | 0.158 | 0.104 | log |
| $\beta_2$ | $-0.002$ | $5.4 \times 10^{-4}$ | physical |
| $\gamma$ | $-1.440$ | 0.146 | physical |
| $\beta_{N1}$ | 0.170 | 0.0625 | log |
| $\beta_{N2}$ | $-0.005$ | $4.1 \times 10^{-4}$ | physical |
| $\gamma_N$ | $-2.000$ | 0.0353 | physical |
| $\beta_{H1}$ | 0.067 | 0.0912 | log |
| $\beta_{H2}$ | 0.000 | $9 \times 10^{-5}$ | physical |
| $\gamma_H$ | $-6.900$ | 1.391 | physical |
| $\delta_1$ | $-2.550$ | 0.435 | physical |
| $\delta_2$ | 1.240 | 0.211 | log |
| $r$ | 0.250 | 0.0545 | log |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | 1.00 | 0.50 | log |

\textbf{SP1, TP2, TP3}

| Parameter | $x^{\mathrm{ctr}}$ | $\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}}$ | Space |
|-----------|-------------------:|------------------------:|-------|
| \textit{SP1} | | | |
| $\alpha$ | 0.09 | 0.50 | log |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$ | 0.095, $-0.00014$, $-1.21$ | 0.20, 0.05, 0.30 | log, phys., phys. |
| \textit{TP2} | | | |
| $\alpha_A, \alpha_H$ | 0.73, 0.0015 | 0.50, 0.50 | log |
| $p_H$ | 0.028 | 1.00 | logit |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$ | 0.095, $-0.00014$, $-1.21$ | 0.20, 0.05, 0.30 | log, phys., phys. |
| \textit{TP3} | | | |
| $\alpha_A, \alpha_S, \alpha_H$ | 0.73, 0.10, 0.0015 | 0.50, 0.50, 0.50 | log |
| $p_S, p_H$ | 0.028, 0.50 | 1.00, 1.00 | logit |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2, \gamma$ | 0.095, $-0.00014$, $-1.21$ | 0.20, 0.05, 0.30 | log, phys., phys. |
| \textit{All three} | | | |
| $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ | 1.00 | 0.50 | log |

---

# Uncalibrated baselines

Before examining what the Finnish data tells the models, it is useful
to know what the models say \emph{without} any Finnish information ---
that is, how well published global parameter sets predict Finnish SOC
stocks. These uncalibrated baseline runs serve as both a diagnostic and
a narrative anchor: they show where calibration starts and how far it
moves the predictions.

## Purpose and scope

Baseline scripts (`run_yasso07_baseline.R`, `run_yasso15_baseline.R`,
`run_yasso20_baseline.R`) run each Yasso model forward at its published
default parameters across all calibration-ready plots. No MCMC, no
posterior --- a single deterministic forward run per plot. Because SP1,
TP2, and TP3 have no published global calibration, no baselines are
produced for them; the natural reference for the simple models would be
their prior centres, but such runs are not currently generated.

Each baseline uses a steady-state initial condition rather than the
68-year pre-run. At published defaults $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}} = 1$ and
$\sigma_{\mathrm{input}} = 1$, so the steady state and the pre-run
endpoint coincide; the simpler steady-state formulation is used for
transparency. The two auxiliary uncertainty parameters are therefore not
exercised in the baseline --- their effect is, by construction, the
identity.

## Parameter choices

The baseline parameter vector for each model combines the published
decomposition rates, transfer fractions, climate-response parameters, and
size-modifier parameters from the source indicated below. Auxiliary
parameters are fixed at $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}} = \sigma_{\mathrm{input}} = 1$.

| Model   | Transfer fractions | Climate / size parameters |
|---------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Yasso07 | Tuomi et al.\ published defaults (\texttt{y07par\_gui.csv}) | Same source |
| Yasso15 | Published Yasso15 defaults (\texttt{y15par.csv}) | Same source |
| Yasso20 | Yasso15 published defaults | FMI MAP (\texttt{Yasso20\_sample\_parameters.rda}) |

For Yasso07 and Yasso15 the baseline is the full published parameter set.
For Yasso20, the climate and size parameters are the FMI MAP values, but
the 12 transfer fractions use the Yasso15 published defaults rather than
the Viskari (2022) Yasso20 fraction values. At the published Yasso20
fractions, the per-pool outflow for two pools marginally exceeds the
available budget; the HIKET solver's diagonal-dominance guard (see the
Fortran-implementation section) classifies this as non-conservative and
the run is numerically unstable. The Yasso15 fractions satisfy the budget
and run stably. This substitution applies only to the Yasso20 baseline;
the Yasso20 calibration (§\ref{param-yasso20}) samples all 12 fractions
freely under the budget constraint and is unaffected.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Status.} The choice of Yasso20 baseline
parameterisation is provisional. An alternative would be to reproduce the
original Yasso20 baseline using the unmodified FMI reference code, which
tolerates the published fractions without the diagonal-dominance guard.
Which option to adopt is left open pending the manuscript narrative.

## Diagnostic outputs

Each baseline produces the following outputs in
`diagnostics/{Model}_baseline/`:

| File | Content |
|------|---------|
| `*_mean_soc_trajectory.png` | Mean predicted SOC $\pm$ 1 SE across plots vs observed campaign means |
| `*_delta_soc_trajectory.png` | Mean annual $\Delta$SOC rate (tC ha$^{-1}$ yr$^{-1}$) vs observed |
| `*_obs_vs_pred.png` | Scatter coloured by vegetation type (`kasvup_tyyppi`) |
| `*_residuals_diagnostic.png` | 6-panel residual diagnostics |
| `*_rf_importance.png` | Random-forest permutation importance of site covariates |
| `*_worst25_timeseries.png` | Time series for the 25 worst-fitting plots |
| `*_residuals_histogram.png` | Distribution of log-scale residuals |
| `*_residuals.csv` | Per-observation residuals |
| `*_plot_ranking.csv` | Per-plot mean absolute log-residual |
| `*_report.txt` | Summary metrics (R$^2$, RMSE, bias, MAPE) and RF results |

The mean absolute SOC and the annual $\Delta$SOC trajectory plots use
the same visual form as the calibrated predictive outputs, allowing
direct side-by-side comparison of baseline and posterior trajectories
in the manuscript figures.

---

# Transient initialisation\label{transient}

## Why initialisation matters for the Finnish data

The observed Finnish SOC trajectory --- accumulating from 1985 through
Biosoil (2006) and approximately stabilising toward Komeetta (2024) ---
is not easily reproduced by a model that starts from an analytically-imposed
steady state in 1985. The reason is straightforward. Under first-order
kinetics with approximately flat litter inputs (as observed in the
plot-level Tupek et al. dataset), a model at steady state in 1985 predicts
approximately flat SOC from that point forward. But the data shows
accumulation. The only way a model at steady state can produce an upward
SOC trend is if litter inputs are also rising --- and the plot-level inputs
do not show a rise of sufficient magnitude.

The implication is that Finnish forests in 1985 were \emph{not} at
equilibrium with their recent litter inputs. Their SOC stocks were below
what the contemporary productivity would sustain at steady state, and
they have been accumulating toward that higher equilibrium ever since.
This is consistent with the management history of Finnish forests: the
post-war decades saw intensive management (clearcutting, drainage,
fertilisation) that left many forest stands in a recovering state by
1985, with carbon stocks still building up.

A calibration that forces $C(1985) = C_{\mathrm{ss}}(\text{contemporary litter})$
has no degree of freedom to fit this accumulation signal. Either it
rejects all models, or it absorbs the discrepancy into implausible
kinetic parameters. The transient initialisation approach removes this
constraint.

## The 68-year pre-run

Instead of computing $C(1985)$ analytically from contemporary inputs,
each MCMC draw runs the model forward 68 years from 1917 to 1985, ending
at a calibrated initial state.

The choice of 1917 (Finland's independence) as the pre-run anchor is
partly practical (a natural reference point for Finnish forestry history)
and partly statistical (68 years is long enough that the fast AWEN pools
are approximately at steady state by 1985 regardless of the start
condition; only H retains meaningful memory of the initial state).

During the pre-run, two inputs must be specified:

\textbf{Climate:} No gridded climate data exist before 1961. The pre-run
therefore uses a constant climate modifier equal to $\xi(\bar{\mathrm{climate}})$
--- the modifier evaluated at the mean of the observed climate window
(1985--2004). This is an approximation, but an appropriate one: the
Yasso climate response is smooth and the mean is a reasonable
representative value for this multi-decadal period.

\textbf{Litter:} Litter inputs are linearly interpolated between a
historical anchor at 1917 and the start of the observed series at 1985:

$$J(i) = J_{1917} + \frac{i-1}{67}\,(J_{1985} - J_{1917}), \qquad i = 1, \ldots, 68.$$

The anchors are parameterised as:

$$J_{1917} = J_{\mathrm{full\,mean}} \cdot \sigma_{\mathrm{init}} \cdot \sigma_{\mathrm{input}},
\qquad
J_{1985}  = J_{t_0\,\mathrm{mean}}  \cdot \sigma_{\mathrm{input}},$$

where $J_{\mathrm{full\,mean}}$ is the mean litter input over the full
observed series and $J_{t_0\,\mathrm{mean}}$ is the mean over the first
five observed years (1985--1989). The pre-run begins at the analytical
steady state under $J_{1917}$ and constant climate.

## The two auxiliary uncertainty parameters

$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ and $\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ are free
parameters in every model, both log-transformed with a prior centred at
1.0 (log-normal, unconstrained-space SD = 0.5, physical 95\% interval
approximately [0.37, 2.72]).

\textbf{$\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$} scales the entire contemporary litter
time series (1985 onward). It absorbs uncertainty in the litter input
model --- plot-level litter estimates carry uncertainty from both the
litter model itself and the representation of understorey vegetation,
which is not individually measured. $\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ also
scales $J_{1985}$, the endpoint of the pre-run interpolation.

\textbf{$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$} scales only the 1917 litter anchor,
$J_{1917}$. It encodes the ratio of historical (pre-1985) to contemporary
mean productivity. The two parameters have strictly non-overlapping roles
in the pre-run formula:

\begin{quote}
$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}} > 1$: forests were historically \emph{more}
productive (old-growth or pre-intensification conditions). The 1985
initial SOC is above what contemporary litter inputs would sustain at
steady state. The model predicts declining SOC from 1985.

$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}} < 1$: forests were historically \emph{less}
productive (post-disturbance recovery). The 1985 initial SOC is below
the contemporary equilibrium. The model predicts accumulating SOC from
1985, consistent with the Finnish observations.

$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}} = 1$: the 1917 litter was equal to the
contemporary series mean; the pre-run approaches the same initial state
as the analytic steady state.
\end{quote}

The posterior on $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ is therefore a direct inference
about the pre-1985 productivity history of Finnish forest plots. A
posterior that favours $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}} < 1$ across models
constitutes model-based evidence for the post-disturbance recovery
narrative.

## Jensen's inequality correction

The steady-state climate modifier used to start the pre-run is computed
as $\xi(\bar{\mathrm{climate}})$, not as $\overline{\xi(\mathrm{climate}_t)}$.
Because $\xi$ is convex in temperature and concave in precipitation,
Jensen's inequality gives $\overline{\xi_t} \geq \xi(\bar{\mathrm{climate}})$;
the bias is 5--15\% under typical Finnish inter-annual variability. Each
model wrapper provides a `compute_xi_mean_*` function that averages the
climate scalars over the steady-state window (first 20 observed years)
and evaluates $\xi$ once at the means, correctly avoiding the average-of-$\xi$
bias.

## Implementation across models

The pre-run protocol is uniform but adapted to each model's solver:

| Model | Pre-run implementation |
|-------|----------------------|
| SP1, TP2 | Analytical 68-step R loop (exact per-step solution) |
| TP3 | Euler 68-step R loop (matches the runtime stepping method) |
| Yasso07/15/20 | 68-row litter input fed to the Fortran solver; terminal per-cohort state extracted via `attr(run, "C_final")` |

For the Yasso Fortran models, the pre-run is implemented as a genuine
Fortran run, not an analytical approximation. The 15-element terminal
state (5 pools $\times$ 3 litter cohorts) is returned via `C_final`,
an output argument added specifically to support this chaining. Without
it, only the 5 summed pool totals would be available, which is
insufficient to correctly initialise the per-cohort bookkeeping for the
forward simulation.

## Likelihood adjustment

In the non-transient version of the engine (retained as a fallback),
$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ inflated the observation standard deviation at
the first observation per plot, acting as a likelihood patch to absorb
initial-state uncertainty. With transient initialisation,
$\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ has already entered the model dynamics; retaining
the likelihood patch would double-count its effect. The engine flag
`transient_init = TRUE` removes this patch.

---

# Likelihood

## Form

Conditional on parameters, observations are modelled as

$$y_{p,t} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(\hat{C}_{p,t},\;
  \hat{C}_{p,t} \cdot \sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}\bigr),$$

where $y_{p,t}$ is observed SOC (tC ha$^{-1}$) at plot $p$, year $t$,
and $\hat{C}_{p,t}$ is predicted total SOC. This is a multiplicative-normal
model: the error standard deviation scales with the prediction, so
relative observation error is constant across the SOC range.

## Fixed $\sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}$

$\sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}$ is held at the empirical coefficient of variation
of the SOC observations across all calibration plots and years
($\approx 0.45$--$0.50$). It is not sampled.

This is a deliberate design choice. If $\sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}$ were
sampled, a structurally inadequate model could inflate it to absorb its
residual variance, hiding structural inadequacy. By fixing
$\sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}$ at the same empirical CV across all six models,
structural differences are forced into the residuals --- which is exactly
where the intercomparison wants them.

## Per-plot log-likelihood

For plot $p$ with observations at $\{t_1, \ldots, t_{n_p}\}$:

$$\log L_p = \sum_{k=1}^{n_p} \log \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(y_{p,t_k};\,
  \hat{C}_{p,t_k},\; \hat{C}_{p,t_k} \cdot \sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}\bigr).$$

Total log-likelihood: $\sum_p \log L_p$ plus the log-Jacobian of parameter
transforms. Plots are evaluated in parallel via `mclapply`.

## Rejection logic

Three hard rejections (return $-\infty$) apply:

1. $\hat{C}_{p,t}$ is non-finite at any observation year.
2. $\hat{C}_{p,t} \leq 0$ at any observation year (undefined in the
   multiplicative-error model).
3. The model run returned `NULL` (Fortran crash or wrapper exception).

No physical-ceiling guard is applied. Implausible predictions yield
large negative log-likelihoods and are rejected by MCMC naturally.
The likelihood evaluates $\hat{C}_{p,t}$ only at observation years;
trajectory values between campaigns are computed but not entered into
the likelihood.

## Holdout split for independent validation

Plots flagged `is_holdout` in `site_raw.csv` are excluded from the
likelihood sum: the calibration is fit only to the non-holdout plots.
The held-out plots are carried forward (via the inputs RDS) to the
predictive stage, where prediction metrics (R$^2$, RMSE, bias, 95\%
coverage) are computed separately for the calibration and holdout sets.
This provides an out-of-sample check that the calibrated models
generalise beyond the plots used to fit them, rather than only
reproducing in-sample fit. The split is applied uniformly across all
six models, using the same `is_holdout` plot assignment so that every
model is validated against the same held-out plots.

---

# MCMC sampling

The Differential Evolution with snooker update sampler (DEzs) is used,
via the `BayesianTools` package (Hartig et al. 2023). DEzs proposes new
states using weighted differences between current chain states, then
accepts/rejects via the standard Metropolis ratio. It mixes well for
moderate-dimensional problems (10--50 parameters) and handles correlated
posteriors better than random-walk Metropolis.

## Settings

Shared across all six models, defined in `calib_config.R`:

| Setting | Value | Note |
|---------|-------|------|
| Chains | 5 | Sufficient for Gelman--Rubin $\hat{R}$ |
| Iterations | 50\,000 | Per chain |
| Burn-in | 5\,000 | 10\% of iterations |
| Thinning | None | Full chain retained |
| Initial state | prior centre $x^{\mathrm{ctr}}$ | All chains seeded at same point |

## Parallelisation

Two levels of parallelism are available:

* **Per-plot, within-chain:** each likelihood evaluation runs the per-plot
  inner loop in parallel via `mclapply` with `CORES_PER_CHAIN` workers.
* **Across-chain:** chains can be launched in parallel on a multi-core
  machine, each running its own per-plot parallelism.

The within-chain mode is mandatory on macOS (nested forking is unsafe).

---

# Sanity checks and diagnostics

## Pre-MCMC sanity check

Before launching MCMC, each calibration script runs a forward simulation
at the prior centre for four randomly selected plots. Three conditions
must hold:

1. All predicted SOC values are finite and positive.
2. Predicted-to-observed ratio lies in $[0.1, 10]$ at every observation year.
3. The likelihood `ll_fn(best_x)` is finite across all calibration plots.

A diagnostic PNG is written (`{Model}_forward_at_defaults_{RUN\_ID}.png`).
Condition 1 or 3 failure aborts the calibration; condition 2 produces a
warning only.

Input diagnostics (`diagnostics_inputs.R`, run separately) confirm that
implied mean residence times (MRT = mean SOC / mean litter) do not exceed
100 years. Plots exceeding this threshold are excluded upstream
(`calib_ready = FALSE`).

## Convergence diagnostics

Computed on post-burnin samples in unconstrained (sampling) space:

| Diagnostic | Threshold | Interpretation |
|------------|-----------|----------------|
| $\hat{R}$ (univariate) | $< 1.05$ | Good per-parameter convergence |
| $\hat{R}$ (multivariate) | $< 1.05$ | Good joint convergence |
| Effective sample size | $> 200$ | Adequate independent draws |
| Acceptance rate | 0.2--0.4 | Reasonable DEzs mixing |

With 20--26 free parameters in the Yasso models and only two to three SOC
observations per plot, poor $\hat{R}$ for individual transfer fractions is
expected --- the fractions are weakly identified by the aggregate SOC
likelihood. This is structural non-identifiability, not a sampler failure.

## Diagnostic outputs

Written to `Calibration_real_data_transient/diagnostics/{Model}/`:

| File | Content |
|------|---------|
| `*_traces_*.png` | Trace plots, one panel per parameter |
| `*_marginals_*.png` | Prior vs posterior KDE (shared x-grid) |
| `*_rhat_barplot_*.png` | $\hat{R}$ per parameter |
| `*_kl_divergence_*.png` | KL divergence posterior $\|$ prior |
| `*_report_*.txt` | Tabular $\hat{R}$, ESS, quantile summaries |
| `*_forward_at_defaults_*.png` | Pre-MCMC sanity check (4 plots) |

Prior and posterior KDE are evaluated on the same x-grid derived from
the prior's 99th-percentile range, so tight priors are not artificially
compressed.

---

# Output files

Written to `Calibration_real_data_transient/runs/`:

| File | Content | Used by |
|------|---------|---------|
| `{Model}_chains_{RUN\_ID}.rds` | BayesianTools chain objects (unconstrained space) | Re-diagnostics |
| `{Model}_posterior_{RUN\_ID}.rds` | Post-burnin samples in physical space | Predictive scripts |
| `{Model}_metadata_{RUN\_ID}.rds` | Run configuration, wallclock, $\hat{R}$, ESS | Reproducibility |
| `{Model}_inputs_{RUN\_ID}.rds` | Per-plot input structures used in calibration | Predictive scripts |

The inputs RDS contains `climate_by_plot`, `inputs_by_plot`,
`litter_means`, `obs_meta`, `plot_info`, `plots_real`, `holdout_plots`,
`sigma_obs_fixed`, `sigma_ppm`, `STEADY_STATE_YEARS`, and the transient
pre-run parameters (`PREINIT_YEAR`, `N_PREINIT_SMOOTH`). It provides
exact provenance: predictive scripts use the same per-plot structures the
calibration used, regardless of any subsequent updates to `Data_work.R`.

---

# Key design decisions

1. **Transient initialisation via a 68-year pre-run.** The 1985 SOC
   trajectory is inconsistent with a steady-state initial condition under
   flat litter inputs. The pre-run from 1917 gives all models a calibrated,
   physically-grounded initial state. $\sigma_{\mathrm{init}}$ is the key
   parameter encoding pre-1985 productivity history; its posterior is
   directly interpretable as evidence for or against a post-disturbance
   recovery narrative.

2. **Uniform pre-run protocol across all six models.** SP1/TP2 use exact
   analytical steps; TP3 uses Euler steps matching its runtime method;
   Yasso07/15/20 use the Fortran solver with `C_final` chaining. The
   protocol produces the same conceptual quantity (initial state at 1985)
   for all models; structural differences in the resulting posteriors are
   not contaminated by initialisation differences.

3. **Six models spanning two design axes.** Pool complexity (SP1 $\to$
   TP2 $\to$ TP3 $\to$ Yasso07) and climate integration (Yasso07 $\to$
   Yasso15 $\to$ Yasso20) vary independently. Conclusions about the
   saturation signal are drawn from the ensemble, not from individual models.

4. **Multiplicative-normal likelihood.** The standard deviation of the
   observation error scales with the prediction, representing proportional
   rather than absolute measurement uncertainty. This is appropriate for
   SOC stocks, where measurement noise and spatial representativeness
   both scale with stock magnitude.

5. **Fixed $\sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}$** at the empirical CV of the SOC
   observations, identical across all six models. Prevents structurally
   inadequate models from absorbing their residual variance into the error
   term; forces differences into the residuals where the intercomparison
   can examine them.

6. **$\xi(\bar{\mathrm{climate}})$ rather than $\overline{\xi(\mathrm{climate}_t)}$**
   for the pre-run climate. Jensen's inequality correction; 5--15\% bias
   in boreal conditions otherwise. Implemented uniformly via
   `compute_xi_mean_*` functions.

7. **Steady-state climate window = first 20 observed years.** The 1985--2004
   climate is used to compute the mean $\xi$ for the pre-run. This
   pre-dates the accelerated boreal warming of the 2010s and is
   representative of the pre-run period.

8. **Decomposition rates fixed at published defaults** for the Yasso
   family ($\alpha_A, \alpha_W, \alpha_E, \alpha_N, p_H, \alpha_H$).
   Reduces free-parameter count and keeps the intercomparison clean.
   SP1, TP2, and TP3 have no published global calibrations; all their
   parameters are free.

9. **All 12 transfer fractions free in all three Yasso models.** The
   Viskari (2022) structural zeros and derived fractions in the original
   Yasso20 are not imposed, because they would give Yasso20 fewer degrees
   of freedom than Yasso07/15 and make the structural intercomparison
   uninterpretable.

10. **Stick-breaking parameterisation with budget $B = 1 - p_H$.**
    Guarantees the per-column mass-budget constraint exactly; the remainder
    of each pool's outflow is respired as CO$_2$.

11. **Weakly informative priors on transfer fractions**
    ($\sigma_{\mathrm{ppm}} = 1.0$ in unconstrained space). Finnish data
    drives pool-flow structure, which is the primary structural-comparison
    axis.

12. **Informative priors on climate and size parameters** (Yasso15/20
    from FMI posterior SDs; Yasso07 from literature CIs). Prevents the
    $\gamma$\,/\,$\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$ compensation loop and the
    $\delta_2$ NaN pathology.

13. **Three SOC observation years (1985, 2006, 2024).** The Komeetta
    campaign (2024) provides a third calibration target. The
    1985--2006 interval constrains the accumulation dynamics; the
    2006--2024 near-stability constrains long-term decomposition rates
    and the saturation behaviour. Full Komeetta data description in the
    data documentation.

14. **SP1, TP2, and TP3 share the Yasso07 $\xi$ formula.** Climate
    response comparison across the pool-complexity axis is not confounded
    by approximation-scheme differences.

15. **ICBM topology for TP2.** All litter enters $A$; $H$ receives only
    transferred mass. Eliminates a weakly-constrained input-partitioning
    parameter and is standard in Nordic forestry SOC models.

16. **Sequential cascade for TP3.** No back-transfers between pools;
    A $\to$ S $\to$ H only. This is the minimal three-pool generalisation
    of TP2, extending the complexity ladder by one step before the full
    AWEN topology of Yasso07.

17. **Global $\sigma_{\mathrm{input}}$.** A single multiplicative litter
    scaling per model run. Per-species or per-region stratification is
    a possible extension but is not used in the current pipeline.

18. **No physical-ceiling rejection** in the likelihood. Implausible
    predictions are penalised via the log-likelihood, not a hard cap.
    Consistent with Viskari (2022).

---

# Software

| Component | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| R | $\geq$ 4.3 |
| `BayesianTools` | Hartig et al. (2023); DEzs sampler |
| `coda` | Gelman--Rubin $\hat{R}$, ESS |
| `compiler` | `cmpfun()` byte-compilation of likelihood |
| `parallel` | `mclapply()` per-plot parallelism |
| `ranger` | Random-forest residual analysis |
| Fortran 90 cores | `R CMD SHLIB`; `.Fortran()` interface (Yasso family) |
| SP1, TP2, TP3 | Pure R; no Fortran |

Random seed: `set.seed(2025)` before chain initialisation, consistent
across all models. Test plot subsampling (when `N_PLOTS_TEST` is set):
`set.seed(42)`.

---

# References

* Andrén, O. \& Kätterer, T. (1997). ICBM: The introductory carbon
  balance model for exploration of soil carbon balances.
  \textit{Ecological Applications} 7, 1226--1236.
* Hartig, F. et al. (2023). BayesianTools: General-purpose MCMC and SMC
  samplers and tools for Bayesian statistics. R package version 0.1.8.
* Tuomi, M. et al. (2008). Leaf litter decomposition -- estimates of
  global variability based on Yasso07 model. \textit{Ecological Modelling}
  220, 3362--3371.
* Tuomi, M. et al. (2009). Heterotrophic soil respiration --- comparison
  of different models on data from litterbag microenvironments.
  \textit{Agriculture, Ecosystems and Environment} 132, 209--217.
* Tuomi, M. et al. (2011). Soil carbon model Yasso07 graphical user
  interface. \textit{Environmental Modelling \& Software} 26, 1358--1362.
* Tupek, B. et al. Litter input dataset for Finnish NFI plots.
  Zenodo. \url{https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19736499}.
* Viskari, T. et al. (2022). Calibrating the soil organic carbon model
  Yasso20 with multiple Finnish datasets. \textit{Geoscientific Model
  Development} 15, 1735--1752.
